coredata.dc:title: Bioaccumulation determines the toxicity of carbon dots to two marine dinoflagellates
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yue-Yue
Li, Jia-Meng
Ji, Rong
Zhang, Hongguo
Zhang, Wei
Miao, Ai-Jun
coredata.dc:description: With the ever-increasing application of carbon dots (CDs), a substantial amount will be released and assemble in the aquatic environment. Nevertheless, potential photodegradation of CDs in the aquatic environment, their accumulation and impacts in aquatic organisms remain unclear. Our study examined the toxicity of CDs to two marine dinoflagellates Prorocentrum micans and Prorocentrum donghaiense. Their bioaccumulation including the uptake and elimination kinetics was also determined. Significant photodegradation of CDs in seawater was observed. Moreover, both the degraded CDs and their photodegradation products were toxic to the dinoflagellates. Although P. donghaiense was more sensitive to CDs than P. micans with the median effect concentration 17.0 and 99.0 mg L−1, respectively, such sensitivity difference disappeared when the toxicity data were plotted against cellularly accumulated CDs instead of their concentration in the experimental medium. Therefore, the higher sensitivity of P. donghaiense was attributable to its higher accumulation of CDs. Overall, the photodegradation and bioaccumulation of CDs should be considered when evaluating their environmental risks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioaccumulation
Carbon dots
Dinoflagellates
Photodegradation
Toxicity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Toxicity of CDs and their photodegradation products

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank two anonymous reviewers for their constructive suggestions on this paper. This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21822605 , 22176093 , and 21677068 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2022300303 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physicochemical properties of CDs. (a) The TEM image and (b) the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs showed their good dispersibility in both deionized water (DI) and seawater (SW). (c) The isoelectric point of CDs was less than one based on the variation of their zeta potential with pH. Data in (c) are mean ± standard deviation (n = 3).

Photodegradation of CDs. (a) A substantial amount of CDs was continuously degraded under light irradiation but much less in the dark. (b) No significant (p > 0.05) difference was observed when comparing the photodegradation of CDs in seawater of different salinities (5, 10, 20, and 35 ppt), while much less CDs were degraded in deionized water (0 ppt). (c) More CDs were degraded at higher pH. Data are mean ± standard deviation (n = 3).

Toxicity of CDs to the dinoflagellates. Prorocentrum micans was more tolerant to (a) CDs and (b) their photodegradation products than P. donghaiense according to the relative change of cell specific growth rate (μ) with the concentration of CDs or the photodegradation products in the experimental medium ([CDs]med). The x-axis of (b) was not the real concentration of the photodegradation products but the concentration of CDs where those degradation products derived from. The cellular concentration of (c) CDs ([CDs]cell) or (d) photodegradation products ([CDs-DP]cell) in P. micans was less than those in P. donghaiense especially at high levels of [CDs]med. (e) When the relative change of μ was plotted against [CDs]cell, all data points from the CD toxicity tests of the two dinoflagellates could be fitted to the same dose-response curve. (f) When the relative change of μ was plotted against [CDs-DP]cell in the toxicity test of the photodegradation products, P. micans was still more tolerant to the photodegradation products than P. donghaiense. Data are mean ± standard deviation (n = 3).

Effects of temperature on the bioaccumulation of CDs. Cellular concentration of CDs ([CDs]cell) in (a) P. donghaiense and (b) P. micans increased linearly with exposure time at 24 °C (Normal) but remained relatively unchanged with exposure time at 4 °C (Cold). Data are mean ± standard deviation (n = 3).

Bioaccumulation kinetics of CDs at different ambient concentrations of CDs. Cellular concentration of CDs ([CDs]cell) in (a) P. donghaiense and (b) P. micans increased linearly with exposure time and also was higher at higher concentrations of CDs in the experimental medium ([CDs]med). (c) The rate of CD uptake by P. micans was lower than that of P. donghaiense when [CDs]med was 100 and 200 mg L−1 while the uptake rates of both dinoflagellates were comparable when [CDs]med was 0.1, 1, and 10 mg L−1. Data are mean ± standard deviation (n = 3).

Efflux of CDs. The efflux of CDs by P. micans was faster than that by P. donghaiense and the efflux of CDs by both dinoflagellates was independent of the concentration of CDs in the experimental medium (1 and 10 mg L−1) during the uptake period. Data are mean ± standard deviation (n = 3).
Chemical components of the artificial seawater.

head.ce:title: Bioaccumulation determines the toxicity of carbon dots to two marine dinoflagellates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, China PRC

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, School of the Environment, Nanjing University, Nanjing, Jiangsu Province, 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, School of the Environment, Nanjing University, Nanjing, Jiangsu Province, 210023, China PRC

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangzhou University, No. 230, Huanxi Road, University Town, Guangzhou, 510006, Guangdong Province, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of the Environment, Nanjing University, Mail box 24, Xianlin Road 163, Nanjing, 210023, Jiangsu Province, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the ever-increasing application of carbon dots (CDs), a substantial amount will be released and assemble in the aquatic environment. Nevertheless, potential photodegradation of CDs in the aquatic environment, their accumulation and impacts in aquatic organisms remain unclear. Our study examined the toxicity of CDs to two marine dinoflagellates Prorocentrum micans and Prorocentrum donghaiense. Their bioaccumulation including the uptake and elimination kinetics was also determined. Significant photodegradation of CDs in seawater was observed. Moreover, both the degraded CDs and their photodegradation products were toxic to the dinoflagellates. Although P. donghaiense was more sensitive to CDs than P. micans with the median effect concentration 17.0 and 99.0 mg L−1, respectively, such sensitivity difference disappeared when the toxicity data were plotted against cellularly accumulated CDs instead of their concentration in the experimental medium. Therefore, the higher sensitivity of P. donghaiense was attributable to its higher accumulation of CDs. Overall, the photodegradation and bioaccumulation of CDs should be considered when evaluating their environmental risks.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), first obtained during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through preparative electrophoresis in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), are carbon nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescence and appreciable biocompatibility (Kang and Lee, 2019). As a rising star in the nanocarbon family, CDs have become a promising photoluminescent nanomaterial with a variety of applications, including sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, photo- and electrocatalysis (Li et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2007). With the ever-increasing production and application of CDs, a substantial amount will be released into the environment and finally assemble in the aquatic environment. Nevertheless, the behavior of CDs in the aquatic environment, their potential accumulation and further impacts in aquatic organisms remain largely unknown.

According to the literature, the toxicity of CDs to aquatic organisms is determined by their physicochemical properties and different organisms show different sensitivity (Kang et al., 2015; Li et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2011; Yu et al., 2005; Liu et al., 2012; Zhao et al., 2011). For instance, positively charged CDs were found to be more toxic to zebrafish embryo than their negative counterparts (Chung et al., 2021). The N-doped CDs were the most toxic to the green alga Chlorella, followed by the N,S-doped and undoped CDs (Xiao et al., 2016). Moreover, Yao et al. (2018) examined the toxicity of CDs to aquatic organisms of different trophic levels. They found that the sensitivity to CDs decreased with the increase of trophic level. Namely, the CD sensitivity decreased in the order of Scenedesmus obliquus (green alga) > Daphnia magna (zooplankton) > Danio rerio (zebrafish). Although CDs were reported to be able to degrade either in the experimental medium (Zhang et al., 2019) or inside the organisms (Zhang et al., 2018), how degradation may affect their toxicity to aquatic organisms needs to be investigated. On the other hand, although significant accumulation of CDs in the algae (Zhang et al., 2019) and zebrafish embryo (Chung et al., 2021) was observed by fluorescence microscopy, no quantitative study was performed due to the lack of suitable methods. Under this condition, potential correlation between the toxicity of CDs and their bioaccumulation remains unexplored.

In the present study, we examined the toxicity of CDs to two marine dinoflagellates Prorocentrum micans and Prorocentrum donghaiense. These two dinoflagellates were chosen because they are the common algal species of red tide with different size (Dou and Goebel, 2001; Yoo et al., 2009). Further, they are mixotrophic and thus have a high potential to internalize nanoparticles (Jeong et al., 2010). Potential photodegradation of CDs in seawater and the contribution of this process to the algal toxicity of CDs were also investigated. Since carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen are the main elements of CDs and these elements are also the major components of algae, it is hard to calculate the bioaccumulation of CDs based on the increase in carbon content of the algal cells. Moreover, although CDs are fluorescent, their fluorescence is not stable under light irradiation during the experimental period. Under this condition, 14C-labeled CDs were synthesized by microwave pyrolysis of 14C-labeled glucose (Zhu et al., 2009). Thereafter, the accumulation kinetics of CDs including their uptake and efflux (synonym for elimination) was determined to reveal the mechanisms underlying the algal toxicity of CDs and further to explain the potential sensitivity difference between the two algal species. Overall, the main aim of the present study is to explore, 1) whether CDs could be photodegraded in seawater, 2) the toxicity of CDs and their photodegradation products to the marine algae, and 3) the bioaccumulation kinetics of CDs in the marine algae and its relationship with the algal toxicity of CDs. The findings of the present study could improve our understandings about the environmental behavior and risks of CDs.

The CDs were synthesized by 30-min microwave pyrolysis of glucose (1.1 g) in the presence of 1.5 mL water and 5 mL polyethylene glycol as the surfactant (Zhu et al., 2009). The dark-brown suspension thus obtained was then diafiltered through a 3-kDa (kDa) regenerated cellulose membrane in a stirred cell (Millipore, USA) to remove the dissolved impurities. The carbon concentration in the CD stock was measured by Elementar vario TOC analyzer (Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH, Germany). The CD concentration throughout the text was thus based on carbon weight. Their morphology in deionized water was visualized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-200CX from JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) (Ryu et al., 2017). Their size distribution in deionized water and seawater as well as their zeta potential in 10 mM NaCl solution under a series of pH (1–8) was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS, ZetaPALS from Brookhaven Instruments, New York, USA). Whenever CD quantification was needed, the radioactive 14C-labeled CDs were applied. They were synthesized following the same procedure as the non-radioactive CDs. The only exception was that 14C(U)-glucose (1 mCi) was added together with the non-radioactive glucose (1.1 g) (Zhu et al., 2009). The radioactivity of the 14C-labeled CDs was measured by a liquid scintillation counter (LSC, Tri-Carb 2800 TR, PerkinElmer, USA).

Axenic cultures of the marine dinoflagellates P. micans (CCMA-190) with the diameter of ∼25 μm and P. donghaiense (CCMA-364) with the diameter of ∼15 μm were obtained from the Center for Collections of Marine Algae, State Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Science, Xiamen University. They were maintained in f/2 culture medium (Ji et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2010) (pH = 8.1 ± 0.1) at 24 °C on a 12 h/12 h light/dark cycle with the irradiance of 60–70 μmol photons m−2 s−1. Unless otherwise stated, these parameters applied to all the experiments below. The seawater used to maintain the stock culture was collected from Xiamen Bay, Fujian, China. It was filtered through a 0.22-μm polycarbonate membrane (Millipore) and then autoclaved before the preparation of culture medium.

To exclude the possible interferences from undefined compounds (e.g., natural organic matter) in natural seawater, artificial seawater (Miao et al., 2010) was used in all experiments below. Its chemical components are listed in Table 1 with the pH of 8.2. Cool-white fluorescent light bulb served as the light source with a light intensity of 60 μmol photons m−2 s−1. A dark treatment without any light irradiation was also included for comparison. The initial concentration of 14C-labeled CDs was 300 mg L−1 and the whole degradation lasted 6 d with 4 time points (0, 2, 4, and 6 d). At each time point, a 0.5-mL aliquot was taken out and passed through a 3-kD ultracentrifuge filter (PALL Nanosep series). The radioactivity of the ultrafiltrate was determined by LSC, using Permafluor cocktail (PerkinElmer) as the scintillator. The radioactivities of the CD suspension and the CDs retained on the membrane at the beginning and the end of the experiment were also measured for mass balance calculation. The proportion of CD degradation was calculated as the radioactivity of the ultrafiltrate divided by total radioactivity of the whole suspension.

Potential effects of salinity on CD photodegradation was also examined. For this purpose, four salinity treatments [5, 10, 20, and 35 parts per thousand (ppt)] were applied and an additional treatment using deionized water as the experimental medium was also included. The other procedure was similar to the degradation experiment above. Besides salinity, the photodegradation of CDs at different pH was also examined. The procedure was mostly similar to the different salinity experiment except that different pH values (i.e., 5.5, 6.5, 8, and 9.5) were applied with the salinity kept at 35 ppt.

Both dinoflagellates were exposed to 0, 3, 10, 30, 100, and 300 mg L−1 of 14C-labeled CDs in duplicate for 6 d. These concentrations were chosen based on our preliminary experiment to obtain complete dose-response curves. The initial cell density ranged from 2 × 104 to 1.2 × 105 cells mL−1 for P. donghaiense and from 1 × 104 to 4 × 104 cells mL−1 for P. micans, respectively. Different cell density was applied to achieve similar final biomass for the different CD concentration treatments and the concentrations of CDs in the medium remained relatively unchanged. During the 6-d experimental period, the cells were enumerated every day by a Z2 Coulter Counter, based on which the cell specific growth rate was calculated (Zang et al., 2018). At the end, 10 mL culture from each replicate was filtered through an 8.0-μm polycarbonate membrane (Millipore) and the CDs weakly associated on the cell surface were rinsed off by fresh seawater without CDs. The filter together with the cells on it was then translocated to 1.5 mL seawater and homogenized in ice-water bath for 5 min (3-sec interval between each 3-sec sonication). Thereafter, the homogenate was mixed with 7 mL Gold Star scintillation cocktail (Meridian) and the radioactivity was measured by LSC. Cellular concentration of CDs ([CDs]cell, pg μm−3 cell volume) including degraded CDs and their degradation products was calculated as follows,
(1)
[ C D s ]
c e l l
=
R a d i o a c t i v i t y
p e r
μ m
3
c e l l
v o l u m e ×
[
C D s
]
m e d
×
10 9
R a d i o a c t i v i t y
p e r
l i t e r
e x p e r i m e n t a l
m e d i u m
Where [CDs]med (mg L−1) represents CD concentration in the experimental medium. Since the two dinoflagellates have different cell size, the bioaccumulation was normalized to cell volume to exclude the effect of cell size. The volumes of these two algal cells were calculated assuming they are spherical.

Besides CDs themselves, potential toxicity of any dissolved chemicals (degradation products) liberated from CDs during the 6-d period in the toxicity test above was also investigated. The whole procedure was similar to the toxicity experiment above. The only exception was that the toxicity media containing 0–300 mg L−1 14C-labeled CDs were first kept in the incubator under the same environmental conditions as the following toxicity test for 6 d. The CDs in the media were then removed by ultrafiltration through a 3-kD regenerated cellulose membrane. Thereafter, the dinoflagellates were inoculated and their growth was monitored for another 6 d. As the pore size of the 3-kD membrane is approximately 1 nm, any nanoparticles should have been removed and only the toxicity of dissolved chemicals liberated from the CDs was examined herein. Possible bioaccumulation of CD degradation products was also quantified by LSC.

To verify the internalization of CDs, their accumulation kinetics in normal dinoflagellate cells and in their cold-treated counterparts was compared. For this purpose, P. donghaiense and P. micans at mid-exponential growth phase were collected by centrifugation (3082×g, 10 min), rinsed with fresh seawater without CDs, and then resuspended into the uptake medium with 1 mg L−1 of 14C-labeled CDs in duplicate. As different from normal cells, the rinse and uptake media for the cold-treated cells had been equilibrated at 4 °C overnight. Subsequent uptake kinetics of this treatment was also performed at 4 °C. The whole experiment lasted 4 h with five time points (0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 h). At each time point, 10 mL cell culture was filtered through an 8.0-μm polycarbonate membrane (Millipore) for the measurement of [CDs]cell in the dinoflagellates, similar to the toxicity experiment above. Radioactivities of the whole cell culture and of the <8.0-μm filtrate were also determined for mass balance calculation. The uptake rate of CDs was calculated as the slope of the linear regression between [CDs]cell and exposure time. Afterward, the uptake kinetics of different concentrations of CDs was examined. For this purpose, both P. donghaiense and P. micans were exposed to 0.1, 1, 10, 100, and 200 mg L−1 of 14C-labeled CDs in duplicate for 4 h at 24 °C. The other procedure was the same as the uptake experiment above.

Both dinoflagellate cells were cultured in f/2 medium containing 1 or 10 mg L−1 of 14C-labeled CDs for 24 h until [CDs]cell arrived at the steady state. Afterward, the cells were collected, rinsed with fresh seawater three times, and resuspended in the efflux medium without any CDs. The whole depuration lasted 48 h with 7 time points (0, 1.5, 3, 6, 12, 24, and 48 h). At each time point the concentration of CDs retained in the cells was measured similar to the uptake experiment above. Then the efflux rate constant (K e, h−1) was calculated using the equation below (Miao and Wang, 2006),
(2)
[ C D s ]
c e l l − t
=
[ C D s ]
c e l l − 0
×
e
−
K e
t
Where [CDs]cell-0 (pg μm−3) and [CDs]cell-t (pg μm−3) represent CD concentration in the cells after 0- and t-h depuration, respectively.

Significant differences (p < 0.05) were determined based on the results of a one-way or two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with post-hoc multiple comparisons (Tukey or Tamhane) (SPSS 11.0 by SPSS, Chicago, USA). The normality (Kolmogorov–Smirnov and Shapiro–Wilk tests) and homogeneity of variance (Levene's test) of the data were examined during the ANOVAs.

The CDs obtained in the present study had an average size of 9.2 nm in deionized water as calculated from more than 1000 particles randomly chosen from the TEM images (Fig. 1 a). Under this condition, their hydrodynamic diameter as measured by DLS was approximately 14.7 nm (Fig. 1b). The size of CDs obtained herein was larger than what was observed in our pervious study (∼3 nm) (Liu et al., 2021) and in Zhu et al. (2009) (2.75–3.65 nm) with similar synthesis procedure. Such discrepancy may be explained by the longer hydrolysis time we applied herein to maximize the yield (38%) (Baker and Baker, 2010). When the CDs were added to seawater, they aggregated quickly with an average hydrodynamic diameter of 79.3 nm (Fig. 1b) after 30 min due to the compression of their double electric layer under high ionic strength (Wang et al., 2020). Thereafter, their size remained constant during the 6-d period. The good dispersibility of CDs in deionized water and seawater could be explained by their highly negative surface potential (∼−30 mV) and thus strong electrostatic repulsion under both conditions with an isoelectric point of less than one (Fig. 1c) due to the enrichment of carboxylic and phenolic functional groups on their surface (Liu et al., 2021). Other physicochemical properties of CDs, including their chemical composition and photoluminescence, could be found in our previous study (Liu et al., 2021).

Similar to our previous observations in human cell culture medium (Liu et al., 2021), significant photodegradation of CDs was also observed in seawater. As shown in Fig. 2 a, a substantial amount of dissolved organic matter was continuously released from CDs under light irradiation but much less in the dark. In the former case, the concentration of dissolved organic carbon in the < 3-kDa fraction increased consecutively from 0.63 mg L−1 at the beginning to 126.9 mg L−1 at the end. Accordingly, ∼42.3% of the CDs initially added was degraded into the dissolved phase (<3 kDa) during the 6-d period. When comparing the photodegradation of CDs in seawater of different salinities (5–35 ppt) but the same pH, no significant (p > 0.05) difference was observed with 41.3–46.9% of the CDs degraded after 6 d (Fig. 2b). Nevertheless, more CDs were degraded in above salinity (5–35 ppt) treatments as compared to those in deionized water, where 23.0% of the CDs initially added were degraded at the end. Considering the pH difference (i.e., 8.1 vs. 5.5) between the seawater of different salinity and deionized water, we further tested the potential effects of pH on CD photodegradation. As shown in Fig. 2c, pH had remarkable effects on the photodegradation of CDs. Accordingly, 24.9%, 23.4%, 42.7%, and 51.6% of CDs were degraded into the < 3-kDa fraction after 6 d of irradiation when the pH of seawater was 5.5, 6.5, 8, and 9.5, respectively. Therefore, the CD photodegradation difference between seawater and deionized water was not due to their difference in ionic strength but due to their difference in pH. These results were consistent with our previous observation that ionic strength had negligible while light intensity, light wavelength, temperature, and pH displayed remarkable effects on CD photodegradation in human cell culture medium (Liu et al., 2021). As hydroxyl and alkyl radicals play important roles in the photodegradation of CDs (Liu et al., 2021; Yue et al., 2019), it could further be speculated that the production of these radicals increased with the increase in pH. This hypothesis was supported by the observations in Guan et al. (2011), where the production rate of hydroxyl radicals was found to increase with pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity of CDs and their photodegradation products
ce:section.ce:para: When P. donghaiense and P. micans were exposed to different concentrations of CDs for 6 d, remarkable toxicity was observed (Fig. 3 a). The growth of P. donghaiense was suppressed by 12.2% and 93.6% in the presence of 10 and 30 mg L−1 of CDs, respectively, and completely ceased thereafter. As compared to P. donghaiense, P. micans was more tolerant. Its cell growth remained relatively unchanged until [CDs]med went up to 30 mg L−1 and decreased continuously afterward with an inhibition of 92.9% in the highest CD concentration treatment (300 mg L−1). The [CDs]med-based median effect concentration (EC50) thus obtained was 17.0 and 99.0 mg L−1 for P. donghaiense and P. micans, respectively.

In our previous study, the photodegradation products of CDs were found to have a major contribution to the cytotoxicity of CDs after light irradiation (Liu et al., 2021). Considering the significant photodegradation of CDs, we then examined the potential contribution of the photodegradation products to the toxicity of the whole CD suspension observed in Fig. 3a. As shown in Fig. 3b, significant inhibition of cell growth was observed when P. donghaiense and P. micans were exposed to the photodegradation products of CDs. Further, P. micans was more tolerant than P. donghaiense, similar to the observations in the toxicity tests of the total CD suspension. The x-axis of Fig. 3b was not the real concentration of the photodegradation products but the concentration of CDs where those degradation products derived from. The EC50 based on the initial concentration of CDs thus obtained was 47.7 and 196.3 mg L−1 for P. donghaiense and P. micans, respectively. These values were higher than those obtained from the toxicity test of the total CD suspension (17.0 and 99.0 mg L−1 in Fig. 3a). Further considering the fact that the photodegradation products of this toxicity test were obtained from the CD suspension after 6-d irradiation, their concentrations should be higher than those in the toxicity test of the CD suspension. In the latter case, the concentrations of the photodegradation products increased continuously with exposure time. These phenomena imply that both the photodegradation products and the degraded CDs themselves had significant contribution to the toxicity of the whole CD suspension as shown in Fig. 3a.

According to the literature, CDs could suppress the algal growth by inhibiting their photosynthesis and nutrient absorption, and acidifying their growth environment (Liu et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2016; Yao et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019). Yao et al. (2018) examined the toxicity of CDs to aquatic organisms of different levels and found that the alga was the most sensitive species with an EC50 of 74.8 mg L−1 after 96-h exposure, which was similar to our observations herein. Similarly, Xiao et al. (2016) investigated the toxicity of CDs with different doping element to the green alga Chlorella. The 96-h EC50 thus obtained was in the range of 38.6–232.5 mg L−1. Zhang et al. (2019) also examined the toxicity of CDs as synthesized by an electrochemical method to Chlorella during a 4-d period with the EC50 of 70 mg L−1. Both studies showed that oxidative stress is an important mechanism underlying the toxicity of CDs to Chlorella (Liu et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2019). Despite the algal toxicity of CDs observed in the present study and in the literature, CDs were found to be able to induce the growth of Chlorella vulgaris when their concentration was relatively low (<10 mg L−1) (Zhang et al., 2018). Such inductive effects were possibly due to the degradation of CDs by C. vulgaris into CO2, which enhanced the photosynthesis of the alga. Overall, CDs including their degradation products could inhibit the algal growth at high concentrations but induce their growth when the concentration was relatively low.

To further explain the sensitivity difference of CDs and their photodegradation products between the two dinoflagellates, the bioaccumulation of CDs and their degradation products at the end of the toxicity tests was determined. As shown in Fig. 3c, [CDs]cell increased hyperbolically with [CDs]med and P. donghaiense accumulated more CDs than P. micans. Accordingly, [CDs]cell was 0.13 and 0.053 pg μm−3 for these two algae, respectively, in the highest CD concentration treatment. Similar to CDs, P. donghaiense also accumulated more CD degradation products than P. micans with the cellular concentration of the degradation products ([CDs-DP]cell) of 0.015 and 0.0061 pg μm−3, respectively, in the highest concentration treatment (Fig. 3d). Moreover, [CDs-DP]cell was approximately one order of magnitude lower than [CDs]cell, suggesting that the degradation products only had a minor contribution to the accumulation of CDs measured in the toxicity test of the total CD suspension. To further explore whether the sensitivity difference between the two dinoflagellates could be explained by their different accumulation of CDs or their degradation products, the relative change of the cell specific growth rate was plotted against [CDs]cell or [CDs-DP]cell instead of [CDs]med. As shown in Fig. 3e, the sensitivity difference between the two dinoflagellates disappeared and all data points could be fitted to the same dose response curve in the toxicity experiment of the whole CD suspension. It suggests that the toxicity difference observed in Fig. 3a was mainly due to the accumulation difference of the two algae. In contrast to the toxicity experiment of the whole CD suspension, the toxicity difference between the dinoflagellates still existed when the toxicity data of the photodegradation products were plotted against [CDs-DP]cell (Fig. 3f), suggesting that the accumulation difference was not the main reason for the sensitivity difference to the degradation products between the two algal species. It further indicates that the photodegradation products may only have a minor contribution to the toxicity observed in the toxicity experiment of the whole CD suspension.

The bioaccumulation results in the toxicity experiment above showed that the higher sensitivity of P. donghaiense to CDs than that of P. micans was mainly attributable to the higher CD bioaccumulation ability of the former alga. According to the biodynamic modeling (Khan et al., 2012), bioaccumulation is the net result of uptake and efflux. To reveal the mechanisms underlying the bioaccumulation difference between the two dinoflagellates, the uptake and efflux kinetics of CDs in P. donghaiense and P. micans were then examined. In the uptake experiment, potential internalization of CDs into the algae was first verified. When the uptake experiment was performed at 4 °C, particle endocytosis ceased as a result of the rigidification of the cell membrane (Chu-Ky et al., 2005) and any accumulation determined in this case was due to passive internalization or the cell surface adsorption of CDs (Wang et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, although a significant amount of CDs was accumulated in both algae at 4 °C, their cellular concentration remained constant during the 4-h exposure period. By contrast, [CDs]cell increased linearly with exposure time at 24 °C. Further considering the comparable size of CDs at both temperatures, the results above imply that CDs could enter the cells of dinoflagellate in an energy-dependent manner. Similar to our observation herein, no internalization of PPEI-EI-coated CDs by the human breast cancer cell MCF-7 was observed at 4 °C in Cao et al. (2007) By contrast, Zhou et al. (2014) compared the uptake of CDs by neural cells at 37 °C and 4 °C. Although CD uptake was reduced at 4 °C revealing an energy-dependent endocytosis of CDs, significant internalization was still seen under this condition. The existence of energy-independent pathways (e.g., passive diffusion) was thus proposed. It seems that different cells or CDs synthesized by different methods had different uptake mechanisms.

The uptake kinetics of different concentrations of CDs in P. donghaiense and P. micans was then examined. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, [CDs]cell increased linearly with exposure time. Further, more CDs were accumulated at higher [CDs]med and P. donghaiense accumulated more CDs than P. micans in the higher CD concentration treatments. Accordingly, [CDs]cell at the end of the uptake experiment was 1.2 × 10−4, 7.6 × 10−4, 3.1 × 10−3, 1.7 × 10−2, and 6.7 × 10−2 pg μm−3 for P. donghaiense and was 1.1 × 10−4, 1.1 × 10−3, 6.8 × 10−3, 1.7 × 10−2, and 1.5 × 10−2 pg μm−3 for P. micans when [CDs]med was 0.1, 1, 10, 100, and 200 mg L−1, respectively. The above trend was more obvious when the uptake rate of CDs was calculated. As shown in Fig. 5c, although both dinoflagellates showed comparable uptake rate of CDs when [CDs]med was 0.1, 1, and 10 mg L−1, the uptake rate of P. donghaiense was much higher than that of P. micans when [CDs]med was 100 and 200 mg L−1. Moreover, a hyperbolic relationship was observed between the uptake rate of CDs (V) and [CDs]med for P. micans, which could be simulated by the Michaelis-Menton equation below (Wang et al., 2014),
(3)
V =
V max
×
[
C D s
]
m e d
K m
+
[
C D s
]
m e d
Where V max represents the maximum uptake rate of CDs when the binding sites on the cell surface were saturated by CDs. K m is the Michaelis-Menton constant indicating the binding affinity (i.e., how strong CDs were associated with the receptors) of CDs with the receptors on the cell surface. The values of V max and K m thus obtained were 2.7 × 10−3 pg μm−3 h−1 and 9.18 mg L−1, respectively. In contrast to P. micans, the uptake rate of CDs increased linearly with [CDs]med for P. donghaiense and no saturation was observed in the range of [CDs]med used herein. Therefore, the higher accumulation ability of P. donghaiense at higher [CDs]med was due to its higher V max. Nevertheless, the binding affinity of CDs with the receptors of P. donghaiense was weaker than with those of P. micans.

Diffusion from the bulk solution to the cell surface is a critical step in the bioaccumulation of various pollutants (including nanoparticles) (Buffle et al., 2009). Regarding CDs as rigid spheres, their diffusion coefficient (D, 1.0 × 10−11 m2 s−1) could be calculated according to the Stokes-Einstein equation as follows (Yang et al., 2014),
(4)
D =
k B
× T
6 π × η
s
×
R H
Where k B (m2 kg s−2 K−1) is the Boltzmann constant. T (K) and η s (kg s−1 m−1) represent the absolute temperature and solvent viscosity, respectively. R H (m) indicates the hydrodynamic radius of the particle as determined by DLS. Their maximum diffusion to the cell surface J max (pg cell−1 h−1) could then be calculated as follows (Buffle et al., 2009),
(5)
J max
= 3.6 ×
10 15
× D × S ×
(
[
C D s
]
m e d
−
[
C D s
]
p
)
×
(
1 R
+
1 δ
)
Where S (m2 cell−1) means the cell surface area on average. [CDs]p (mg L−1) represents CD concentration in the cell periphery. R (m) and δ (m) signify the radius of the alga and its diffusion layer thickness, respectively. Assuming that nanoparticle uptake by the cells was rather quick (i.e., [CDs]p = 0 mg L−1) and δ was much larger than R (Buffle et al., 2009), then J max was calculated to be in the range of 1.9 × 10−4–0.39 pg μm−3 h−1 and 6.9 × 10−5–0.14 pg μm−3 h−1 for P. donghaiense and P. micans, respectively. It was approximately 11.3–49.4 times higher than the actual CD uptake rate of P. donghaiense, suggesting CD uptake by this alga was not diffusion limited. By contrast, J max was 2.9–4.4 times higher than the actual CD uptake rate of P. micans in the three lowest CD concentration treatments, but 25.2 and 58.3 times higher when [CDs]med was 100 and 200 mg L−1, respectively. It implies that CD uptake by P. micans was possibly diffusion limited only at low levels of [CDs]med.

In summary, 14C-labeled CDs were synthesized in the present study to examine their accumulation and toxicity in dinoflagellates. CDs showed remarkable toxicity to the dinoflagellates P. donghaiense and P. micans as a result of the combined effect from the degraded CDs and their photodegradation products with molecular weight <3 kDa. Although P. donghaiense was more sensitive to CDs than P. micans, such sensitivity difference disappeared when the toxicity results were plotted against cellularly accumulated CDs instead of their concentration in the experimental medium. Therefore, the higher sensitivity of P. donghaiense was attributable to its higher accumulation of CDs, which resulted from its higher uptake but lower efflux of CDs as compared to P. micans. Our findings could improve our understanding about the potentially environmental risks of CDs.

Yue-Yue Liu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft preparation. Jia-Meng Li: Methodology. Rong Ji: Methodology, Resources. Hongguo Zhang: Formal analysis, Software. Wei Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing-Reviewing and Editing. Ai-Jun Miao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing-Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank two anonymous reviewers for their constructive suggestions on this paper. This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21822605, 22176093, and 21677068) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2022300303).
